title: Social Behavior
description: How animals interact with each other
expanded_description: An introduction to social behavior—how animals communicate, court, cooperate, and compete with each other. This guide explains why animals live in groups, how they send signals to each other, why males often have fancy feathers or antlers, and why animals sometimes help others even at a cost to themselves.

core_concept.principle: Social behavior is how animals interact with each other—communicating, competing, cooperating, and caring for young. These behaviors evolve because they help animals survive and reproduce. Sometimes animals help relatives because they share genes; sometimes they cooperate with others because they might get help in return.
core_concept.explanation:
Animals don't live in isolation. They interact with each other constantly—fighting, mating, warning of danger, sharing food, raising young. These interactions are social behavior.

Why do animals live in groups?
• Safety in numbers: More eyes to spot predators. A lone zebra is easy prey; a herd is harder to attack.
• Easier to find food: Wolves hunt together to bring down large prey.
• Help raising young: Meerkats take turns babysitting pups.
• Staying warm: Emperor penguins huddle together in the Antarctic cold.

But living in groups also has costs—competition for food, fights over mates, disease spread. Animals balance the benefits and costs.

Why do animals help each other? Sometimes it's selfish: helping relatives (who share your genes) helps your genes survive. Sometimes it's a trade: I help you today, you help me tomorrow. Sometimes it's just safer to be in a group.

In this section, we'll explore how animals communicate, how they attract mates, how they organize their societies, and why cooperation evolves.

image.alt: Wolf pack hunting, meerkats standing guard, birds in flock, bees in hive. Labels: communication, cooperation, competition, care for young.
image.caption: Social behavior: Animals communicate, cooperate, compete, and care for young. Living in groups has benefits (safety, food, help) and costs (competition, disease).

sections.section_title: Introduction: Why Do Animals Live Together?
sections.introduction:
Look at a herd of zebras, a flock of birds, a pack of wolves, or a colony of ants. These animals are living together—they are being social.

But why? Why would an animal share space, food, and mates with others? Wouldn't it be easier to be alone?

Benefits of group living:
• Safety from predators: More eyes to spot danger. A lone bird is easy prey; a flock has many lookouts.
• Easier hunting: Wolves and lions hunt together to catch prey they couldn't catch alone.
• Help raising young: Many birds and mammals have helpers that assist parents.
• Finding mates: Being in a group means potential mates are nearby.
• Sharing information: Bees tell each other where flowers are; birds learn from others where to find food.

Costs of group living:
• Competition: More animals fighting for the same food, water, mates.
• Disease: Close contact spreads illness.
• Attracting predators: A big group is easier to spot.
• Conflict: More fights over who's in charge.

Animals balance these costs and benefits. In good times, groups may grow. In hard times, animals may spread out.

In this section, we'll explore how animals communicate, how they attract mates, how they organize their societies, and why they sometimes help each other.

introduction_image.alt: Zebra herd (safety), wolf pack (hunting), meerkats (raising young), bees (information sharing).
introduction_image.caption: Benefits of group living: Safety in numbers, easier hunting, help raising young, sharing information.

key_concept.definition: Social behavior includes all interactions between animals of the same species—communication, courtship, cooperation, and competition. These behaviors evolve because they help animals survive and reproduce.
key_concept.context:
Why do some animals live in huge colonies while others live alone? It depends on their environment. Animals that face many predators often live in groups (safety in numbers). Animals that eat scattered food may live alone (less competition). Animals that can hunt together may form packs (wolves, lions).

Key questions in social behavior:
• How do animals send messages to each other?
• How do they choose mates?
• How do they organize their groups?
• Why do they sometimes help others?

Real-world connection: Understanding animal social behavior helps us protect endangered species. Wolves hunt in packs—if we break up the pack, they can't hunt. Meerkats rely on group sentinels—if the group is too small, they're vulnerable to predators.

image.alt: Icons: communication (sound waves), courtship (peacock), cooperation (ants), competition (deer fighting).
image.caption: Key aspects of social behavior: Communication (sending signals), Courtship (attracting mates), Cooperation (helping each other), Competition (fighting for resources).

sections.key_concept: Animals communicate with sounds, colors, smells, and movements. They court mates with displays and dances. They live in groups with social structures like pecking orders. And sometimes they help each other—even at a cost to themselves.

classifications.value: Understanding how animals send messages to each other
characteristics: Visual signals: Bright colors (peacock tail), body postures (dog showing teeth), movements (bee waggle dance)
Acoustic signals: Bird songs, whale songs, frog calls, alarm calls
Chemical signals: Pheromones (ants leaving trails, moths attracting mates, dogs marking territory)
Tactile signals: Grooming, touching, vibrations (spiders on webs)
classifications.behavior:
Animals send signals to communicate danger, food, territory, and mating readiness. These signals evolve because they help the sender and receiver—or sometimes because the sender manipulates the receiver.

**Honest signals**: Some signals are costly to produce, so they can't be faked. A peacock's tail is heavy and attracts predators—only a healthy male can afford it. So a big tail is an honest signal of good health.

**Deceptive signals**: Some animals cheat. Fireflies of one species mimic the mating signals of another species—then eat the males that come looking for a mate. Some birds give false alarm calls to scare others away from food.

**Pheromones**: Chemical signals that can travel long distances and last a long time. Ants leave trails to food. Moths can detect a female's pheromone from miles away. Dogs sniff each other's urine to learn about health, sex, and mood.

examples.process: Bee Waggle Dance: Telling Others Where Food Is
examples.explanation:
Honey bees have one of the most sophisticated communication systems in the animal kingdom:
(1) When a bee finds a good patch of flowers, it returns to the hive and performs a "waggle dance" on the honeycomb.
(2) The dance tells other bees: direction (angle relative to the sun), distance (duration of the waggle), and quality (how enthusiastic the dance is).
(3) Other bees watch, learn, and fly straight to the flowers.

Why this matters: This is symbolic communication—the dance isn't a flower, it's a symbol representing a flower. For a long time, this was thought to be uniquely human. But bees do it too!

image.alt: Bee dancing on honeycomb; diagram showing direction and distance encoded; other bees following.
image.caption: Bee waggle dance: A bee tells others where flowers are. The dance encodes direction (angle from sun) and distance (duration of waggle). Symbolic communication.

classifications.type: Courtship and Mating: Attracting a Partner
classifications.value: Understanding how animals find and choose mates
characteristics: Male competition: Males fight for access to females (deer with antlers, elephant seals fighting)
Female choice: Females choose males based on displays, ornaments, songs (peacock tail, bird song)
Courtship displays: Elaborate dances, gift-giving, nest-building
Mating systems: One male, one female (monogamy); one male, many females (polygyny); one female, many males (polyandry); many males, many females (promiscuity)
classifications.behavior:
In most species, females invest more in offspring (eggs are expensive, gestation takes time). Males can mate with many females. This leads to:
• Males competing for females (bigger, stronger, more weapons)
• Females choosing the best males (bright colors, complex songs)

Why are males often more colorful? Because females choose males with bright colors. A bright male is signaling: "I'm healthy enough to survive despite being obvious to predators. My genes are good."

Why do males fight? Because the winner gets more mates. Elephant seal males fight for control of a beach with many females. The winner may mate with 50 females; most males never mate at all.

examples.explanation:
The peacock's tail is one of the most famous examples of sexual selection:
(1) The tail is huge—over half the bird's length. It's heavy, hard to carry, and attracts predators.
(2) Only healthy, strong males can grow a big, beautiful tail and survive despite the burden.
(3) Peahens (females) choose males with the most eyespots, the biggest tails.
(4) Why? Because a male who can survive with such a handicap must have good genes. Her chicks will inherit those good genes.

Why this matters: The peacock's tail seems like a bad idea for survival—but it's great for reproduction. Evolution doesn't care about survival; it cares about passing on genes.

image.alt: Peacock with fanned tail; peahen looking; diagram showing handicap principle.
image.caption: Peacock tail: A costly, dangerous ornament. Only healthy males can afford it. Females choose the most impressive males—they pass on good genes to their chicks.

classifications.type: Social Groups: From Families to Supercolonies
classifications.value: Understanding how animals organize their societies
characteristics: Dominance hierarchies (pecking orders): Who's in charge? Higher-ranking animals get first access to food, mates, and other resources.
Territoriality: Animals defend an area against others of their species. Birds sing to claim territory; wolves mark boundaries with urine.
Cooperative breeding: Helpers assist parents in raising young (meerkats, wolves, some birds).

Eusociality: The highest level of social organization—reproductive division of labor (queen lays eggs, workers do everything else), overlapping generations, cooperative brood care. Examples: ants, bees, termites, naked mole-rats.
classifications.behavior:
Animals live in groups in many different ways:

**Dominance hierarchies (pecking orders)**: In a group of chickens, there's a clear ranking. The top hen eats first, pecks others, gets the best spot to sleep. Lower-ranking hens wait their turn. This reduces fighting—everyone knows their place.

**Territoriality**: Many animals defend an area against others of their species. Birds sing to say "this is mine." Wolves mark boundaries with urine. This ensures the animal has enough resources without having to fight every intruder.

**Cooperative breeding**: In some species, young adults stay with their parents and help raise the next batch of siblings. Meerkats take turns babysitting, guarding against predators, and foraging. Helpers gain experience and eventually inherit the territory.

**Eusociality**: The ultimate social system. Ant colonies have millions of workers that never reproduce—they spend their lives serving the queen. How can this evolve? Workers are sisters—they share 75% of their genes (in bees and ants, sisters are more related than mothers and daughters). By helping the queen produce more sisters, they pass on more genes than if they tried to reproduce themselves.

examples.explanation:
Ant colonies are among the most complex societies on Earth:
(1) A single queen lays all the eggs. She may live for decades.
(2) Workers are sterile females. They forage, build the nest, care for the young, defend the colony.
(3) Drones are males. They do nothing but mate with the queen and die.

How can this evolve? Why would workers give up reproduction?
• In ants, bees, and wasps, females are more related to their sisters (75%) than to their own daughters (50%).
• So a worker can pass on more genes by helping her mother produce sisters than by having her own daughters.
• This is called kin selection—helping relatives is a way to pass on genes.

Ant colonies can be enormous—some supercolonies stretch for miles and contain billions of ants. Yet they function as a single superorganism, with workers sacrificing themselves for the colony.

image.alt: Ant colony: queen, workers, drones; diagram showing relatedness (sisters 75%); supercolony map.
image.caption: Ant colonies: Queen lays eggs; workers (sterile females) do everything. Workers are more related to sisters (75%) than to daughters (50%)—so helping the queen passes on more genes.

principles.principle: Helping Relatives Is a Way to Pass On Genes
principles.statement: Sometimes animals help others even at a cost to themselves. This can make sense if the animals are relatives. By helping a relative survive and reproduce, an animal helps pass on copies of its own genes.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Ground squirrels give alarm calls that warn relatives—even though the caller might attract a predator.
• Wolves and meerkats help raise their siblings.
• Ant workers give up reproduction entirely to help their mother.

**Everyday example**: If you save your niece from drowning, you don't pass on your own genes directly. But your niece shares 25% of your genes. By saving her, you help those genes survive.
principles.example: Belding's ground squirrels give alarm calls when they see a predator. The caller attracts attention to itself—but warns the group. Females (who stay near relatives) give more alarm calls than males (who leave the group). Why? Because females are warning their sisters, daughters, and nieces. Males are warning unrelated squirrels. The benefit of saving relatives outweighs the risk.
principles.analogy: Helping relatives is like investing in a family business. Even if you don't get a salary, if the business succeeds, your family (and your genes) benefit.

image.alt: Ground squirrel giving alarm call; female vs. male calling; relatedness diagram.
image.caption: Kin selection: Ground squirrels give alarm calls. Females (who live near relatives) call more than males (who leave). Warning relatives helps pass on shared genes.

principles.principle: Cooperation Can Evolve Even Among Non-Relatives
principles.statement: Sometimes animals help unrelated individuals. This works if help is returned in the future—"I'll scratch your back if you scratch mine."
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Vampire bats share blood with roost-mates who failed to feed.
• Cleaner fish remove parasites from larger fish—and the larger fish don't eat them.
• Monkeys groom each other—and later get help in fights.

**Everyday example**: If you help a friend move, you expect they'll help you someday. If they never help back, you stop helping. This works when you interact repeatedly and can remember who helped.
principles.example: Vampire bats need blood every 2-3 days. If a bat fails to feed, it may starve. Bats that have extra blood share with hungry roost-mates. But they remember who shared with them. A bat that shares today is more likely to get help tomorrow. This is reciprocal altruism—cooperation based on "you help me, I'll help you."
principles.analogy: Reciprocal altruism is like a potluck dinner. You bring food, others bring food. Everyone benefits. If someone shows up empty-handed repeatedly, they stop getting invited.

image.alt: Vampire bats sharing blood; diagram of reciprocity; cleaner fish with client.
image.caption: Reciprocal altruism: Vampire bats share blood with hungry roost-mates. They remember who shared—and share with those who shared before. "You help me, I'll help you."

principles.statement: Living in groups means conflict—over food, mates, and status. Animals have evolved ways to resolve conflicts without constantly fighting, which wastes energy and risks injury.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Chickens have a pecking order (dominance hierarchy).
• Wolves show submission by licking the dominant wolf's mouth.
• Deer lock antlers and push—but rarely fight to the death.

**Everyday example**: In a classroom, there's usually a social hierarchy. People don't fight over every seat—they learn who sits where. Animals do the same.
principles.example: Red deer stags fight for control of females. But fights are usually ritualized. They size each other up, walk parallel, roar. If one is clearly smaller, it retreats. Only evenly matched stags actually fight—and even then, they push with antlers rather than stab. This saves energy and prevents serious injury.
principles.analogy: Animal conflict is like a staring contest. You try to intimidate the other person. If you can't, you might actually fight. But you'd rather win without fighting.

image.alt: Red deer stags sizing each other up; chickens with pecking order; wolves showing submission.
image.caption: Animals resolve conflicts without constant fighting: Dominance hierarchies, ritualized displays, submission signals. Fighting is costly—better to win without fighting.

components.definition: Social behavior includes communication (signals), courtship (mate attraction), social structures (groups, hierarchies, families), and cooperation (helping, sharing).
components.equation: Social behavior evolves when benefits > costs
components.interpretation: Animals live in groups, cooperate, and help others when the benefits (safety, food, help, mating opportunities) outweigh the costs (competition, disease, conflict).

image.alt: Icons: communication (sounds), courtship (dance), social structure (hierarchy), cooperation (sharing).
image.caption: Key components: Communication, Courtship, Social structures, Cooperation.

categories.description: Young animals play—chasing, wrestling, tumbling. Play helps them learn social skills, practice hunting, and build physical strength.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Play is common in mammals and some birds. It's most common in young animals, especially those that live in social groups. Play has costs (energy, risk of injury), so it must provide benefits.

Benefits of play:
• Practice hunting skills (lion cubs stalk and pounce on each other).
• Learn social rules (play fighting teaches what's acceptable).
• Build physical strength and coordination.
• Strengthen social bonds.

examples.explanation: Lion cubs spend hours wrestling, stalking, and pouncing on each other. This looks like fun—and it is—but it's also training. Cubs that play more become better hunters as adults. They learn to stalk, ambush, and coordinate with siblings. Play also establishes dominance relationships without serious fighting.

image.alt: Lion cubs wrestling, pouncing, stalking each other.
image.caption: Play: Lion cubs learn hunting skills, social rules, and build strength through play. Play is training for adulthood.

categories.description: Some animals learn from others in their group. This can lead to traditions—behaviors passed down through learning, not genes.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Cultural transmission is when animals learn behaviors from others. This is different from instinct (which is genetic) and individual learning (which is trial and error).

Examples of animal culture:
• Chimpanzees in different regions use different tools—they have tool-using traditions.
• Whales have different songs in different oceans; new songs spread like fads.
• Japanese macaques learned to wash sweet potatoes from one female; the behavior spread through the troop and was passed to offspring.

examples.explanation:
Chimpanzees in different parts of Africa use different tools:
• In Gombe, they use sticks to fish for termites.
• In Tai Forest, they use rocks to crack nuts.
• In some places, they use leaves as sponges to drink water.

These are not genetic differences—they are learned traditions. Young chimps watch adults and practice for years. This is culture—behavior passed down through learning, not genes.

Why this matters: For a long time, culture was thought to be uniquely human. But chimpanzees, whales, and other animals show that learning from others is widespread.

image.alt: Chimpanzees using sticks (termite fishing) and rocks (nut cracking); map showing different tool use across Africa.
image.caption: Animal culture: Chimpanzees have different tool-use traditions in different regions. Young learn from adults—culture passed through learning, not genes.

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Meerkat Helpers
worked_examples.problem:
Meerkats live in groups of 10-50. The dominant pair does most of the breeding. Subordinates (often older offspring) help raise the pups—babysitting, feeding, and guarding against predators.
(1) Why do helpers help instead of leaving to start their own families?
(2) How does this benefit their genes?
(3) What might happen if a helper left to breed on its own?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the helpers and their relationship to the pups.
step_by_step.calculation: Helpers are usually offspring from previous litters. They are full siblings of the new pups (r = 0.5) or half-siblings (r = 0.25). They are helping raise their brothers and sisters.

step_by_step.explanation: Apply the idea of helping relatives.
step_by_step.calculation: By helping raise siblings, helpers pass on genes indirectly. A helper who raises 2 siblings gains as much indirect fitness as having 1 offspring (since siblings share 50% of genes).

step_by_step.explanation: Consider the alternative: leaving to breed alone.
step_by_step.calculation: If a helper leaves, it must find a territory, find a mate, and raise pups alone. In the harsh desert environment, this is risky. Many dispersers die before breeding. By staying, helpers gain safety in numbers and eventually inherit the territory when the dominant pair dies.

Cost of helping: delayed reproduction, risk of predation. Benefit: siblings survive, helpers gain experience, eventual inheritance of territory. For most helpers, staying yields more lifetime offspring than leaving. This is why cooperative breeding evolves.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Helpers stay because leaving to breed alone is risky; they gain indirect fitness by raising siblings. (2) Siblings share 50% of genes—helping raise siblings passes on genes indirectly. (3) Dispersal is risky; many die before breeding. Staying increases survival and eventual breeding opportunities.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Helping relatives can be a good strategy when independent breeding is risky. By raising siblings, helpers pass on shared genes—and eventually inherit the territory.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: How a Dominance Hierarchy Forms
sections.introduction: When animals live together, they need to figure out who's in charge. Dominance hierarchies reduce fighting by establishing a clear pecking order.

process_flow.description: When animals first meet, they may fight or display to assess each other.
process_flow.mechanism: Animals size each other up—size, strength, condition. They may fight, threaten, or display.
process_flow.result: Initial assessment; winners and losers determined

image.alt: Two wolves facing off, sizing each other up.
image.caption: Step 1: Initial encounters. Animals assess each other—size, strength, condition. Fights or displays establish who wins.

process_flow.description: After encounters, animals know their place. Higher-ranking animals get priority access to resources.
process_flow.mechanism: Dominant animals display confidence; subordinates show submission (lowered posture, avoiding eye contact).

image.alt: Dominant wolf eating first; subordinate waiting. Submissive posture (tail down, ears back).
image.caption: Step 2: Rank established. Dominant animals get priority. Subordinates show submission. Fighting decreases.

process_flow.description: Hierarchies are maintained through occasional displays, not constant fighting.
process_flow.mechanism: Dominant animals may threaten subordinates who step out of line. Subordinates avoid challenging dominants unless they have a chance to win (e.g., when dominant is old or sick).

image.alt: Dominant wolf showing teeth; subordinate looking away. Occasional displays maintain order.
image.caption: Step 3: Maintaining the hierarchy. Occasional displays remind everyone of their place. Constant fighting is avoided.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about how scientists study social behavior, including the hormones that influence bonding and the genes that affect sociality.

process_types.energy_profile: Oxytocin is a hormone that plays a role in social bonding, trust, and parental care. It's sometimes called the "love hormone."
process_types.direction: Touch, grooming, bonding → Oxytocin release → Strengthens social bonds → More grooming, more bonding
process_types.function: Understand the biological basis of social bonding
goals: Learn that hormones influence social behavior
Understand why touching and grooming strengthen bonds
Appreciate that animal behavior has a biological basis

primary_example.explanation:
Prairie voles are one of the few mammals that form lifelong pair bonds. When they mate, oxytocin (in females) and vasopressin (in males) are released. These hormones make the voles bond—they prefer to be with their partner rather than a stranger.

If scientists block oxytocin, female voles don't bond. If they give oxytocin to a female that hasn't mated, she bonds with the nearest male!

Why this matters: This shows that social bonding has a chemical basis. The same hormones influence human bonding—oxytocin is released when mothers nurse their babies, when people hug, when couples fall in love. We are not so different from voles.

image.alt: Prairie voles cuddling; diagram of oxytocin release; experiment showing bonding.
image.caption: Oxytocin and bonding: Prairie voles form lifelong pair bonds. Oxytocin (females) and vasopressin (males) trigger bonding. Block the hormone—no bond. Add the hormone—bond with the nearest vole.

image.alt: Human mother nursing baby; couple hugging; oxytocin molecule.
image.caption: Oxytocin: The "love hormone." Released during hugging, nursing, bonding. Same in voles and humans.

process_types.type: The Prisoner's Dilemma: Game Theory in Animal Behavior
process_types.energy_profile: Game theory models how animals make decisions when the outcome depends on what others do. The Prisoner's Dilemma shows why cooperation can be hard to evolve—but also how it can succeed.
process_types.direction: Two animals face a choice: cooperate or defect. Mutual cooperation gives moderate reward; mutual defection gives low reward; defecting while partner cooperates gives high reward. In a single interaction, defection always pays more. In repeated interactions, cooperation can evolve.
process_types.function: Understand why animals sometimes cooperate and sometimes cheat
goals: Learn that decision-making depends on what others do
Understand why cooperation can be hard to evolve
Appreciate that repeated interactions favor cooperation

primary_example.explanation:
Vampire bats need blood every 2-3 days. If a bat fails to feed, it may starve. Bats that have extra blood share with hungry roost-mates. But they remember who shared with them.

This is a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma:
• If both share, both survive (cooperate).
• If one shares and the other doesn't, the cheater gets free blood (defect).
• If neither shares, both may starve (defect).

In a one-time interaction, cheating is best. But bats interact repeatedly. A bat that never shares will be left to starve. So they share—and they remember who shared with them. This is called tit-for-tat: start by cooperating, then do what the other did last time.

image.alt: Vampire bats sharing blood; payoff matrix; tit-for-tat diagram.
image.caption: Prisoner's Dilemma: Vampire bats share blood. In a one-time interaction, cheating pays. In repeated interactions, cooperation evolves. Bats remember who shared—they reciprocate.

image.alt: Payoff matrix for Prisoner's Dilemma; tit-for-tat strategy; computer tournament results.
image.caption: Game theory: The Prisoner's Dilemma shows why cooperation is hard. In repeated games, tit-for-tat (cooperate first, then copy partner) wins.

sections.relationship: Social behavior is how animals interact with each other. Animals live in groups for safety, hunting, and raising young. They communicate with sounds, colors, smells, and dances. They court mates with displays and competitions. They organize into hierarchies and families. Sometimes they help each other—helping relatives passes on shared genes; helping others can lead to help in return. From the peacock's tail to the ant colony, social behavior shows how evolution shapes interactions. Understanding social behavior helps us understand our own—and reminds us that we are part of the animal kingdom.

summary:
Social behavior is how animals interact with each other—communicating, courting, competing, cooperating.

Why live in groups? Benefits: safety in numbers, easier hunting, help raising young, finding mates, sharing information. Costs: competition, disease, attracting predators, conflict.

Communication: Animals send signals—visual (colors, postures), acoustic (songs, calls), chemical (pheromones), tactile (grooming). Honest signals are costly to produce (peacock tail); deceptive signals trick others (firefly mimics).

Courtship and mating: Males often compete for females (elephant seals fight). Females often choose males with elaborate displays (peacock tail, bird song). This is sexual selection—traits that help get mates evolve even if they hurt survival.

Social structures:
• Dominance hierarchies (pecking orders) reduce fighting.
• Territories are defended areas that provide resources.
• Cooperative breeding: helpers assist parents (meerkats, wolves).
• Eusociality: the ultimate social system—ants, bees, termites. Queens reproduce; workers are sterile. Workers help because they are more related to sisters (75%) than to daughters (50%).

Why help others?
• Kin selection: Helping relatives passes on shared genes. Hamilton's rule: help when relatedness × benefit to recipient > cost to helper.
• Reciprocal altruism: Helping non-relatives when help may be returned. Vampire bats share blood with roost-mates; they remember who shared with them.

Examples:
• Bee waggle dance: Bees tell others where flowers are—symbolic communication.
• Peacock tail: Costly ornament that signals good genes.
• Ant colonies: Workers sacrifice reproduction to help the queen—kin selection at its extreme.
• Meerkats: Helpers raise siblings, gaining indirect fitness and eventually inheriting territory.
• Prairie voles: Oxytocin and vasopressin trigger pair bonding—same hormones that influence human bonding.

Game theory: The Prisoner's Dilemma models cooperation. In one-time interactions, cheating pays. In repeated interactions, cooperation can evolve (tit-for-tat). Vampire bat blood sharing is an example of repeated cooperation.

Social behavior evolves because it helps animals survive and reproduce. From the simplest group to the most complex colony, social behavior is shaped by the same forces that shape all of evolution.
